title: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(h) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.29: NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.29' running on TLS metadata from Stream, certificate inventory, nis2_crypto_policy.csv (min_tls_version, allowed_ciphers), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: TLS metadata from Stream, certificate inventory, `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` (min_tls_version, allowed_ciphers)
app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)
spl:
index=stream sourcetype="stream:tls" earliest=-24h
| stats latest(tls_version) as tls_version latest(cipher) as cipher values(expiry_epoch) as cert_expiry by dest_ip dest_port
| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map dest_ip OUTPUT service_name nis2_in_scope
| where nis2_in_scope="true"
| eval weak_tls=if(match(tls_version,"SSLv|TLSv1\.0|TLSv1\.1"),1,0)
| eval cert_problem=if(cert_expiry < relative_time(now(),"+30d@d"),1,0)
| where weak_tls=1 OR cert_problem=1
| table service_name, dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, cert_problem
description: Appropriate use of cryptography protects network and information system security. This use case monitors TLS versions, weak cipher suites, and certificate expiry for public and internal services supporting NIS2-scoped workloads — aligning with organisational cryptography standards.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIS2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes stream and sourcetypes stream:tls. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Enable passive TLS inspection on critical VLAN mirrors; (2) map IPs to NIS2 services; (3) alert on weak protocols and imminent expiry; (4) integrate with PKI renewal workflow; (5) document exceptions with compensating controls. Indexes required: `index=stream`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=stream:tls`; Lookups: `nis2_scoped_service_map`.
visualization: Table (weak TLS services), Timeline (certificate expiry), Single value (critical expiries), Bar chart (by cipher family).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name, All_Certificates.dest | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: TLS metadata from Stream, certificate inventory, `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` (min_tls_version, allowed_ciphers).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Enable passive TLS inspection on critical VLAN mirrors; (2) map IPs to NIS2 services; (3) alert on weak protocols and imminent expiry; (4) integrate with PKI renewal workflow; (5) document exceptions with compensating controls.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=stream sourcetype="stream:tls" earliest=-24h
| stats latest(tls_version) as tls_version latest(cipher) as cipher values(expiry_epoch) as cert_expiry by dest_ip dest_port
| lookup nis2_scoped_service_map dest_ip OUTPUT service_name nis2_in_scope
| where nis2_in_scope="true"
| eval weak_tls=if(match(tls_version,"SSLv|TLSv1\.0|TLSv1\.1"),1,0)
| eval cert_problem=if(cert_expiry < relative_time(now(),"+30d@d"),1,0)
| where weak_tls=1 OR cert_problem=1
| table service_name, dest_ip, dest_port, tls_version, cipher, cert_problem
```

Understanding this SPL

**NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture (Art. 21(2)(h))** — Appropriate use of cryptography protects network and information system security. This use case monitors TLS versions, weak cipher suites, and certificate expiry for public and internal services supporting NIS2-scoped workloads — aligning with organisational cryptography standards.

Documented **Data sources**: TLS metadata from Stream, certificate inventory, `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` (min_tls_version, allowed_ciphers). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: stream; **sourcetype**: stream:tls. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=stream, sourcetype="stream:tls", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **dest_ip dest_port**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where nis2_in_scope="true"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **weak_tls** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **cert_problem** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where weak_tls=1 OR cert_problem=1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name, All_Certificates.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**NIS2 Cryptography Policy Compliance — TLS and Certificate Posture (Art. 21(2)(h))** — Appropriate use of cryptography protects network and information system security. This use case monitors TLS versions, weak cipher suites, and certificate expiry for public and internal services supporting NIS2-scoped workloads — aligning with organisational cryptography standards.

Documented **Data sources**: TLS metadata from Stream, certificate inventory, `nis2_crypto_policy.csv` (min_tls_version, allowed_ciphers). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Certificates.All_Certificates` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: cert_expiry, cert_problem, cipher, dest_ip, dest_port, service_name, tls_version, weak_tls.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (weak TLS services), Timeline (certificate expiry), Single value (critical expiries), Bar chart (by cipher family).

This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
